Saga Pattern — interactive SAGA_PATTERN.md saga_pattern.py

A long distributed transaction as a chain of local transactions T1..Tn, each with a compensating Ci. If step Tk fails, run C(k-1)..C1 in reverse. Click any step below to move the failure point, then Run saga — watch the green forward chain light up, then the orange compensations roll back. The default (ChargeCard fails) matches saga_pattern.py Section A / GOLD CHECK.

① The saga pipeline — click a step to fail it, then run forward + reverse compensation

What to do: click any step to mark it as the failure point (red ring), then press Run saga. Forward steps light up green (→); the failed step turns red ✗; then completed steps roll back to orange (compensated, ←). The wallet drops by the sum of cancellation fees — proof that compensation ≠ undo.
wallet $1000
done (forward OK) failed compensated forward arrow → compensation arrow ←

② Orchestration vs Choreography — two ways to coordinate the same saga

Toggle the two coordination styles. Orchestration: a central brain calls each service in order and drives compensation (Temporal / Camunda / AWS Step Functions). Choreography: no coordinator — each service reacts to the previous one's events; on failure the compensation cascades backwards through subscriptions. Both reach the same final state. Failure = ChargeCard (step 4).
Key difference: orchestration centralizes the flow in one greppable state machine (but adds a central dependency); choreography distributes it across event subscriptions (decoupled, but the overall picture is hidden across services).

③ Compensation ≠ undo — the cancellation-fee gap

What this shows: a DB rollback restores the exact prior value (net $0). A saga compensation refunds the charge minus a cancellation fee (net < 0). After a fully rolled-back saga the wallet is $900, not $1000 — the $100 gap is the irreducible real-world cost. That gap is why sagas are tricky.
Total NET cost of a fully-rolled-back saga = $100 (sum of fees). A true rollback would be $0 — but cross-service rollback is impossible (no shared lock).

④ Saga vs 2PC — eventual consistency vs blocking ACID

The trade-off. 2PC gives ACID across services but blocks (a stalled coordinator freezes everyone holding PREPARE locks). Saga gives up isolation (partial states visible) for non-blocking availability. Microservices pick saga because you cannot hold a cross-service lock.

⑤ Gold check — full pipeline recomputed live in JS

What this proves: the page re-runs the whole booking saga in your browser — fail ChargeCard, run reverse compensations — on the identical logic as saga_pattern.py GOLD CHECK, and checks that orchestration + choreography reach the same final state. The green check: OK below confirms the JS replay matches the .py exactly.
check: … JS saga matches saga_pattern.py GOLD CHECK (final_wallet=900, compensation_count=3, comp_order=[CancelCar,CancelHotel,CancelFlight]).
